summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical overview of all seven ASTM-classified additive manufacturing processes, from FDM to sheet lamination, before comparing two consumer 3D printers: the MakerBot Replicator+ (FDM) and Form 3 (SLA). Frazelle's hands-on testing reveals the Form 3 produces significantly higher quality prints in less time, though the MakerBot offers better user experience with its iPad app and camera integration. The article reflects her characteristic approach of doing deep research before purchasing, wanting 'the best' thing that 'just works' as an integrated product.
key_points: The seven ASTM-classified additive manufacturing processes each have distinct tradeoffs in materials, precision, speed, and applications
SLA (stereolithography) produces smoother, more detailed prints than FDM but traditionally had brittleness limitations
The Form 3 outperformed the MakerBot Replicator+ in both print quality and speed across all test prints
MakerBot's software experience was frustrating with clunky apps and poor cloud sync, while Form Labs' setup was smoother
Future 3D printing improvements should focus on automatic support removal and reliable quality monitoring
money_quotes: I like to do a lot of research and I tend to want the best thing.

What I was personally looking for was a product, meaning something easy to set up, easy to use, and including a fully integrated experience between the hardware and the software. I didn't want something I would have to maintain or debug since I would rather this just work.
Kinda feels like what I would expect an app written by devs with hardware expertise, rather than software expertise, would feel like.

The ability to go from a digital file to a physical object rapidly with many different materials can enable so many folks to create something they could only imagine in their wildest dreams until now.
Obviously having two 3D printers is a bit unseemly so I decided after I tried them both I would donate one to a nearby school.
key_insight: When evaluating hardware products, the integrated software experience matters as much as the underlying technology, and SLA's superior print quality and speed make it the better choice for those seeking polished results over raw hackability.
